title: Coccothrinax guantanamensis
intro: Coccothrinax guantanamensis, also known as the Guantánamo palm, is a species of palm tree endemic to Cuba, specifically found in the Guantánamo Province. This plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin irritations, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Coccothrinax guantanamensis include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Coccothrinax guantanamensis is often prepared as a tea, infusion, or decoction to treat various ailments, although further research is necessary to fully understand its efficacy and potential side effects.
taxonomy: Coccothrinax guantanamensis is classified as a species of palm tree in the genus Coccothrinax. It is native to Cuba and belongs to the family Arecaceae. This palm tree is further categorized as an evergreen and is known to thrive in tropical regions. The classification of Coccothrinax guantanamensis places it within the larger group of monocots, specifically within the subfamily Coccothrinacoideae. The specific classification of Coccothrinax guantanamensis is Coccothrinax guantanamensis (León) León.

The plant's leaves, bark, and roots have been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including fever, digestive problems, and skin conditions.
